Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 214 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 3.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Looking at the image after LEFT, the player (green) moved left, and the white strip moved left too. The dark gray object and blue bar seem to have shifted left as well. Let me check the exact positions.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state after LEFT
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    if n['color'] not in ['w', 'g']:
        print(f"color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)}), children={n['children']}")

# Compare with before LEFT
if last_transition:
    before_seg = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
    print("\nBefore LEFT:")
    for n in before_seg['nodes']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        if n['color'] not in ['w', 'g']:
            print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)}), children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Very interesting! After LEFT:
- Before: White strip at (32,28)-(32,31), horizontal 1x4 strip
- After: White strip at (32,24)-(35,24), vertical 4x1 strip!
The white strip rotated from horizontal to vertical! And the player rotated too:
- Before: Player at (33,28)-(35,31), 3x4 rectangle
- After: Player at (32,25)-(35,27), 4x3 rectangle
Also, the dark gray object at (28,28)-(31,31) changed color from G to c (charcoal)! So it reverted from dark gray back to charcoal.
This is a key insight: LEFT/RIGHT rotates the white strip and player, and also changes the color of dropped objects.
Wait, let me re-examine. The dark gray object was at (28,28)-(31,31) with color G. After LEFT, it's at the same position but with color c. So the LEFT action changed the color of the dark gray object back to charcoal.
Hmm, but that doesn't make sense. Maybe the LEFT action caused the white strip to rotate, and the rotation affected nearby objects?
Actually, looking more carefully:
- Before: White strip at (32,28)-(32,31), horizontal
- After: White strip at (32,24)-(35,24), vertical
The white strip moved from cols 28-31 to col 24, and from row 32 to rows 32-35. So it rotated 90 degrees clockwise and moved left.
The player moved from (33,28)-(35,31) to (32,25)-(35,27). So the player also rotated.
The dark gray object at (28,28)-(31,31) changed to charcoal. This might be because the white strip passed through it or collided with it during the rotation.
This is getting complicated. Let me think about what the goal might be.
Actually, wait. Looking at the image, the white strip is now vertical on the left side of the player. The player is a 4x3 rectangle. The dark gray object is now charcoal again.
Maybe the goal is to:
1. Rotate the white strip to vertical
2. Push it into the charcoal squares to absorb them
3. Drop them as dark gray
Or maybe the goal is to arrange the objects in a specific pattern.
Let me try a different approach. Instead of trying to understand all the mechanics, let me focus on what changes the game state in a meaningful way. The key observations so far are:
1. Arrow keys move the player and white strip
2. White strip absorbs objects it collides with
3. SPACE drops absorbed content as dark gray
4. LEFT/RIGHT can rotate the white strip
Let me try to absorb the left charcoal square at (28,16) by moving the white strip there. The white strip is now vertical at (32,24)-(35,24). The charcoal square is at (28,16)-(31,19).
If I move LEFT, the white strip moves to col 20. Then LEFT again to col 16, overlapping the charcoal square.
But wait, the white strip is vertical (4x1), and the charcoal square is 4x4. So the white strip will overlap with the left column of the charcoal square.
Let me try moving LEFT twice to reach the charcoal square.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# After LEFT:
# - White strip vertical at (32,24)-(35,24)
# - Player at (32,25)-(35,27)
# - Charcoal square at (28,16)-(31,19)
# - Dark gray object reverted to charcoal at (28,28)-(31,31)
#
# LEFT rotated the white strip from horizontal to vertical
# Let me try moving LEFT again to reach the charcoal square at (28,16)
# White strip at col 24, charcoal at cols 16-19
# Moving LEFT moves 4 cols, so white strip goes to col 20
# Then another LEFT moves to col 16, overlapping charcoal
#
# But first, let me check if the white strip is now vertical and can absorb differently
action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
